summary: O'Neil investigates Michigan's 'Emergency Manager' law, which allows state-appointed officials to replace elected local governments in financially distressed municipalities. Prompted by Rachel Maddow's coverage of the Flint water crisis, she maps which Michigan cities are under receivership. By overlaying these locations with census data showing Black population distribution, she reveals a striking visual correlation. The cities stripped of their elected officials are predominantly Black communities, suggesting a racial dimension to this 'suspension of democracy.'
key_points: Michigan's Emergency Manager law allows appointed officials to replace elected local governments, effectively suspending democratic representation
The Flint water crisis, where poisonous water was pumped to save money, occurred under an Emergency Manager
Visual comparison of receivership cities with census data reveals these municipalities have predominantly Black populations
Both individual cities and Wayne County (which includes Detroit) have been placed under receivership
money_quotes: Instead of having elected officials, some specially chosen towns have instead 'Emergency Managers,' who do things like save money by pumping in poisonous water.
What is the racial make-up of the towns who are in receivership?
key_insight: Michigan's Emergency Manager law disproportionately strips democratic representation from Black communities, as demonstrated by mapping receivership cities against census racial demographics.
